title: Auth & Sessions at Scale
description: Authentication is where security and system design collide. This quiz contrasts stateful sessions with stateless JWTs, then digs into the consequences: where to store tokens (`httpOnly` cookie vs. `localStorage` and the XSS/CSRF tradeoff), refresh-token rotation, revocation and logout-everywhere (the hard part of stateless auth), short-lived access tokens, and multi-device/multi-tab coordination. Grounded in the specs: RFC 7519 (JWT), RFC 6749 (OAuth 2.0), RFC 7636 (PKCE), RFC 9700 (OAuth Security BCP), and OpenID Connect Core.

questions.title: What is the defining tradeoff of a stateless JWT versus a server-side session?
questions.description: A server-side session stores state on the server and hands the client an opaque session id; a JWT is self-contained and signed.

options.text: The server validates a JWT from its signature alone — no per-request datastore lookup — but loses the ability to instantly invalidate a token before it expires.
A JWT is encrypted by default, so its payload is hidden from the client; a session id is sent in plaintext.
Server-side sessions cannot scale horizontally because the session can only live on the single server that created it.
JWTs remove the need for HTTPS, since the signature already prevents an attacker from reading or tampering with the token.

questions.explanation:
The core JWT bargain is **statelessness for revocability**. A standard JWT (a JWS) is *signed*, so any server holding the verification key can trust the claims without a shared session store — great for horizontal scale. The price: there is no central record to delete, so you can't instantly kill a valid token; it stays usable until `exp`.

The distractors are classic misconceptions:

- A plain JWT is **base64url-encoded, not encrypted**. Anyone can decode the payload — never put secrets in it (use JWE if you truly need encryption).
- Sessions **do** scale horizontally via a shared store (Redis, a database) or sticky sessions.
- The signature provides *integrity*, not *confidentiality*. Without HTTPS the token can be read in transit and replayed, so TLS is still mandatory.
questions.references: [RFC 7519: JSON Web Token](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc7519) · [OWASP: JWT for Java Cheat Sheet](https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/JSON_Web_Token_for_Java_Cheat_Sheet.html)

questions.title: A JWT is three base64url segments joined by dots: `header.payload.____`. Name the third segment.
questions.description: Type the name of the final part of a compact-serialized JWT (a JWS).
questions.explanation:
A compact JWT is `base64url(header) . base64url(payload) . signature`. The signature is computed over the encoded header and payload (e.g. `HMAC-SHA256(base64url(header) + "." + base64url(payload), secret)` for `HS256`), which is exactly why tampering is detectable: change a single byte of header or payload and the signature no longer verifies.

Note what this does **not** give you: the header and payload are merely encoded, so they are fully readable by anyone who holds the token. The signature protects *integrity and authenticity*, not *confidentiality*.

questions.title: Which statements about where you store an auth token in the browser are correct?
questions.description: Pick every statement that is true. Think about what JavaScript can reach and what the browser sends automatically.

options.text: A token in `localStorage` is readable by any JavaScript running on the page, so a single XSS bug can exfiltrate it.
An `httpOnly` cookie is not exposed to JavaScript, which blunts token theft through XSS.
The browser attaches cookies to matching requests automatically, and that automatic sending is exactly what opens cookie-based auth to CSRF.
Putting the token in `localStorage` instead of a cookie makes the app immune to both CSRF and XSS.

questions.explanation:
There is no storage location that is safe against everything — you choose *which* attack class to fight.

- **`localStorage`**: reachable by any script on the origin, so it is squarely in the XSS blast radius. It is *not* auto-attached to requests, so it sidesteps CSRF (you send it explicitly, e.g. in an `Authorization: Bearer` header).
- **`httpOnly` cookie**: invisible to JavaScript, so an XSS payload can't simply read it out. But cookies ride along automatically on cross-site requests, which is the very mechanism CSRF abuses.

The false option is the trap: moving to `localStorage` trades CSRF exposure for *worse* XSS exposure — it is not immune to both. The honest summary is "cookie ⇒ defend CSRF; `localStorage` ⇒ defend XSS," and you must still defend XSS regardless, because XSS can ride a cookie-authenticated session even when it can't read the cookie.
questions.references: [MDN: Using HTTP cookies — Security](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Guides/Cookies#security) · [OWASP: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)](https://owasp.org/www-community/attacks/csrf)

questions.title: You authenticate with a session cookie. Which measure most directly mitigates CSRF?
questions.description: CSRF tricks the victim's browser into sending an authenticated request the user didn't intend. What stops the cross-site request from carrying valid credentials — or from being honored?

options.text: Set `SameSite=Lax` (or `Strict`) on the cookie and/or require an anti-CSRF token the attacker's page cannot read.
Add the `httpOnly` flag to the cookie.
Respond with `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` so CORS governs every request.
Serve the site over HTTPS and enable HSTS.

questions.explanation:
CSRF works because the browser *automatically* attaches the session cookie to a cross-site request. The two standard defenses attack that directly:

- **`SameSite=Lax`/`Strict`** tells the browser not to send the cookie on cross-site requests (Lax still allows top-level GET navigations), so the forged request arrives *without* credentials.
- **Anti-CSRF tokens** (synchronizer token / double-submit) require a secret value the attacker's origin cannot read, so it can't forge a valid request.

Why the others fail:

- **`httpOnly`** hides the cookie from JavaScript (an XSS defense). It does nothing about CSRF, where no one needs to *read* the cookie — the browser just sends it.
- **CORS** governs whether script may *read a cross-origin response*. It does not block the request from being *sent and processed*; HTML forms and simple requests fly regardless, and `*` actually loosens things (and can't be combined with credentials).
- **HTTPS/HSTS** protect against eavesdropping and downgrade (MITM), an orthogonal threat.

questions.title: With refresh-token rotation, a client presents a refresh token that was already used (and rotated away). What should the server do?
questions.description: Rotation means each refresh token is single-use: spending it returns a new access token **and** a new refresh token, retiring the old one.

options.text: Treat it as suspected theft: a retired refresh token reappearing implies a leaked copy, so revoke the entire token family / session.
Silently issue a fresh access token — refresh tokens are meant to be reused as many times as needed.
Honor the request but emit a log warning; nothing should be revoked on a mere replay.
Extend the current access token in place without issuing a new refresh token.

questions.explanation:
This is **automatic reuse detection**, the security payoff of rotation. Each refresh token is single-use, so legitimately there should only ever be *one* live refresh token in a family. If a previously-rotated token is presented again, two parties hold copies — almost certainly because one was stolen — and the server can't tell the thief from the victim. The safe response is to **invalidate the whole family**, forcing a fresh login and cutting off the attacker (the victim's next refresh fails too, which is the intended, fail-closed behavior).

The distractors describe non-rotating refresh tokens (reusable) or a passive log-and-allow stance — both forfeit the reason rotation exists. RFC 9700 explicitly recommends rotation with reuse detection for public clients that can't keep a client secret.

questions.title: Why keep access tokens short-lived (minutes) while refresh tokens live for days?
questions.description: Access tokens are the credentials sent on every API call; refresh tokens are exchanged occasionally to mint new access tokens.

options.text: A stateless access token can't be revoked instantly, so a short TTL bounds the damage window of a leaked one; the refresh exchange is where revocation checks actually bite.
A shorter expiry makes the signature cryptographically stronger and harder to forge.
Short-lived tokens are physically smaller, which meaningfully reduces request bandwidth.
A short lifetime prevents CSRF because the token expires before an attacker can act.

questions.explanation:
Short-lived access tokens are a **blast-radius control**. Because a stateless JWT is honored on signature alone, you can't reliably yank it mid-flight; a 5-15 minute TTL means a stolen access token is useless soon after capture. Meanwhile the long-lived refresh token does the heavy lifting *through a checkpoint you control*: every refresh is a chance to consult server state — is this user disabled? did they log out? was the family revoked? — so revocation propagates at roughly the access-token TTL.

The distractors are unrelated mechanisms: expiry has nothing to do with signature strength (that's the algorithm and key) or token size (the `exp` claim is a few bytes either way), and CSRF is about automatic credential sending, not token lifetime.

questions.title: You use stateless JWT access tokens and need "log out everywhere" to take effect quickly. Which approaches actually accomplish that?
questions.description: Select every approach that genuinely forces existing access tokens to stop working promptly. Notice the common thread among the ones that work.

options.text: Keep access tokens short-lived and revoke the refresh tokens; live access dies within one short TTL.
Maintain a server-side denylist of revoked token ids (`jti`) and check it on every request.
Store a per-user token version and reject any token whose embedded version is stale after you bump it.
Have the server delete the JWT from the user's browser `localStorage`.

questions.explanation:
The lesson hiding in the three correct answers: **to revoke a stateless token, you must reintroduce some state.** A pure signature check can't know a token is unwanted, so every workable approach adds a lookup —

- **short TTL + revoke refresh**: the cheapest; you accept a small window equal to the access-token lifetime and never check a denylist per request.
- **`jti` denylist**: precise and immediate, but every request now does a (typically cached) revocation lookup — you've partly rebuilt sessions.
- **token version / `tokenVersion` claim**: bumping the user's version invalidates all their outstanding tokens at once ("log out everywhere"), at the cost of reading the current version on each request.

The false option is wishful: a server has **no way to reach into a browser's `localStorage`**, and even if it could, the attacker may have copied the token elsewhere. Revocation must be enforced where the token is *checked* (the server), not where it happens to be stored (the client).

questions.title: What does OpenID Connect (OIDC) add on top of OAuth 2.0?
questions.description: A common interview trap: OAuth 2.0 and OIDC are related but solve different problems.

options.text: An identity layer: a signed **ID Token** (a JWT) describing the authenticated user, plus a standardized `/userinfo` endpoint — OAuth 2.0 by itself is about authorization, not authentication.
Mandatory encryption of all access tokens issued by the authorization server.
A replacement for OAuth's access tokens with long-lived API keys.
Removal of the redirect-based authorization step, so no browser round-trip is needed.

questions.title: Why do public clients (SPAs, mobile apps) use the Authorization Code flow with PKCE instead of the old Implicit flow?
questions.description: PKCE = Proof Key for Code Exchange. The client generates a random `code_verifier`, sends its hash as `code_challenge` up front, then reveals the verifier when redeeming the code.

options.text: PKCE binds the authorization code to a secret only the original client knows (`code_verifier`), so a stolen code can't be exchanged; the Implicit flow instead returned tokens directly in the redirect URL, where they leak.
PKCE lets a public client safely embed a long-lived client secret in shipped source code.
Implicit flow is actually more secure, and PKCE only trades that security away for faster logins.
PKCE end-to-end encrypts the access token so the browser never sees it in cleartext.

questions.explanation:
Public clients can't keep a client secret (anyone can read an SPA's JS or decompile an app), so the classic confidential-client protection is unavailable. PKCE replaces it with a **per-request dynamic secret**:

```text
code_verifier  = random high-entropy string (kept in the client)
code_challenge = base64url(SHA-256(code_verifier))   // sent on the authorize request
```

The authorization server records the `code_challenge`, returns a code via redirect, and at the token endpoint requires the matching `code_verifier`. An attacker who intercepts the *code* (e.g. via a malicious app registered on a custom URI scheme, or browser history) still can't redeem it — they don't have the verifier.

The **Implicit flow** is the foil: it returned the access token straight in the URL fragment (`#access_token=...`), exposing it to browser history, logs, and referrer leakage, with no exchange step to protect. That's why current guidance (RFC 9700) is: Authorization Code + PKCE for everyone, and don't use Implicit. The distractors misstate PKCE — it doesn't enable storing a secret, isn't a security downgrade, and provides no token encryption.
questions.references: [RFC 7636: Proof Key for Code Exchange](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc7636) · [RFC 9700 §2.1: Protecting Redirect-Based Flows](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc9700#section-2.1)

questions.title: Your fleet validates JWTs and intermittently rejects *freshly issued* tokens as "not yet valid" or "expired," depending on which server handles the request. Most likely cause and fix?
questions.description: The errors point at the `nbf` (not-before) and `exp` (expiration) claims, and they correlate with *which* instance validates the token, not with the token's age.

options.text: Clock skew between the issuer and the validating servers; allow a small leeway when checking `exp`/`nbf` and keep all clocks synced via NTP.
The signing key was rotated; re-sign every outstanding token to fix it.
The tokens exceed the maximum allowed JWT size; shrink the payload.
CORS preflight requests are delaying calls past expiry; disable CORS.

questions.explanation:
`exp` and `nbf` are **absolute timestamps** (NumericDate — seconds since the Unix epoch) compared against each validator's *own* clock. If the issuer's clock is even a few seconds ahead, a token it just minted can look like it's `nbf`-in-the-future to a validator whose clock lags — "not yet valid." The mirror case makes a near-expiry token look already `exp`-ired on a fast-running instance. The tell in this scenario is that the outcome depends on *which server* validates, not on elapsed time.

Standard remedy, two parts:

1. **Sync clocks** with NTP so drift stays tiny.
2. **Allow a small leeway** (a few seconds to a couple of minutes) when comparing `exp`/`nbf`; most JWT libraries expose a `clockTolerance`/`leeway` option for exactly this. RFC 7519 explicitly permits "some small leeway, usually no more than a few minutes, to account for clock skew."

The distractors don't fit the symptom: a rotated key yields *signature* failures, not time-validity errors; JWT size limits surface as transport/header errors; and CORS is unrelated to `exp`/`nbf` evaluation.

questions.title: Your SPA is open in three tabs. One tab refreshes the access token, rotating the refresh token. How should the other tabs avoid a stale token *and* a refresh stampede?
questions.description: All tabs share the same origin and the same stored tokens. Rotation makes the old refresh token single-use.

options.text: Coordinate across tabs — e.g. a `BroadcastChannel`/`storage` event or a Web Lock — so exactly one tab performs the refresh and the others pick up the new token.
Let every tab refresh independently at the same time; rotation will sort out the duplicates.
Switch token storage to `sessionStorage` so each tab keeps its own isolated token.
Turn off refresh-token rotation so simultaneous refreshes are harmless.

questions.explanation:
With rotation, **concurrent refreshes are dangerous**, and this is where it bites in practice. If two tabs spend the *same* refresh token at once, the second one is presenting an already-rotated token — which the server's reuse detection reads as theft and responds to by **revoking the whole family**, logging the user out of every tab. So the fix is cross-tab coordination:

- a **Web Lock** (`navigator.locks.request`) so only one tab holds the "refresh" lock at a time, or
- a **`BroadcastChannel`** / the `storage` event to elect one refresher and fan the new token out to the others (often with a single-flight in-memory promise so overlapping calls await one network request).

The distractors each backfire: independent simultaneous refreshes are exactly what triggers reuse-detection revocation (option 2); `sessionStorage` is per-tab, so tabs wouldn't share the session at all, breaking single sign-on across tabs (option 3); and disabling rotation "solves" the race only by discarding the security benefit you adopted rotation for (option 4).

questions.title: Which registered JWT claim is a unique identifier for an individual token — the natural key for building a revocation denylist?
questions.description: Type the three-letter claim name from RFC 7519's registered claims.
questions.explanation:
`jti` is the **JWT ID**: a value that uniquely identifies the token and "can be used to prevent the JWT from being replayed" (RFC 7519 §4.1.7). Because it's unique per token, it's the natural key for a revocation **denylist** — store the `jti` of any token you want to kill (until its `exp`, after which it dies on its own), and reject matches on each request.

Mind the tradeoff it implies: consulting a `jti` denylist on every request reintroduces server state and a per-request lookup — you've partially rebuilt the stateful session you tried to avoid, which is the recurring tension of stateless auth (see the logout-everywhere question). It's a deliberate, often-worthwhile exchange for precise, immediate revocation.
